translation: Ta, Seen. These are the verses of the Qur'an and a clear Book
commentary:
طسٓ ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْقُرْءَانِ وَكِتَابٍ مُّبِينٍ

The Surah opens with mysterious letters - طس - followed by a declaration: "These are the ayahs of the Quran and a clear Book." Unlike the previous Surah An-Naml, which emphasized warnings, Surah An-Naml shifts perspective toward the blessings of Allah ﷻ, the good news for believers and the remarkable gifts bestowed upon the prophets.

The opening requires careful attention to the relationship between "Quran" and "Kitab" (Book). The word Quran inherently refers to something recited aloud - you can do qira'ah without paper in front of you, reciting from memory. Tilawah, by contrast, traditionally implies following along with written text. Though these terms became interchangeable over time, the Quran emphasizes that revelation was primarily an auditory experience for the first generation of Muslims. They heard it rather than read it.

By pairing these concepts - تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْقُرْءَانِ وَكِتَابٍ مُّبِينٍ - Allah ﷻ tells the audience something profound: what you're hearing recited is actually documented in its original form. This becomes especially significant when we remember that the Prophet ﷺ was unlettered (ummi). In a close-knit society like Mecca where everyone knew everyone's background and education, the appearance of letter names in revelation was startling. Letters are meaningless to someone who never learned to read. How did an unlettered man suddenly know ط, س? 

The answer points directly to revelation's divine source. These ayahs aren't just being recited - they're coming from a clear, documented book. The word مُبِين emphasizes clarity and the act of clarification itself.

translation: As guidance and good tidings for the believers
commentary:
هُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

The positive tone continues: this revelation came as guidance and good news specifically for the believers. While the previous Surah focused heavily on warnings, here Allah ﷻ immediately highlights the Quran's role in providing direction and glad tidings to those who embrace faith.

translation: Who establish prayer and give zakah, and of the Hereafter they are certain [in faith]
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ

Who are these believers? They establish prayer, give zakah, and possess absolute conviction regarding the Hereafter. Notice the emphasis: وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ - "and they, regarding the Hereafter, they have certainty." The repetition of "they" stresses this quality of yaqin (conviction).

While the previous Surah discussed most people not believing, this Surah immediately describes those who actually do believe. But why does Allah ﷻ specifically emphasize conviction in the Afterlife among all aspects of faith?

Human beings respond to incentives. At a philosophical level, some argue they want to be good simply because goodness is right, not because of Paradise or fear of Hell. "I'm too evolved for such motivations," they might claim. But can any society function on this principle alone? Human history proves overwhelmingly that motivations and deterrents are essential for social order. Try running a society without consequences for breaking laws and see what happens.

The Quran acknowledges different levels of motivation. The highest is pure love of Allah ﷻ - doing good solely for His pleasure, which we call ihsan. Surah Al-Baqarah, ayah 177, addresses this beautifully: it mentions belief in Allah ﷻ first, then belief in the Last Day. The highest motivation is belief in Allah ﷻ alone. But the Quran doesn't dismiss those motivated by Paradise and Hell - it validates this as legitimate.

When actions are mentioned in the Quran - establishing prayer, giving zakah - the overwhelming motivation for sustained action is conviction in the Afterlife. With absolute certainty in what comes next, giving zakah becomes easy. Leaving business for prayer becomes natural. Why? Because you're convinced you're getting paid far more by Allah ﷻ than any business deal could offer. You're depositing directly into your akhirah account.

The word يُوقِنُونَ implies certainty built gradually through experience. Each sign, each miracle brings them closer to conviction - like the plagues sent to Pharaoh's people, where one sign after another kept inching them toward iman.

translation: Indeed, for those who do not believe in the Hereafter, We have made pleasing to them their deeds, so they wander blindly
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ زَيَّنَّا لَهُمْ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فَهُمْ يَعْمَهُونَ

Now the contrast: those who don't believe in the Afterlife - We have beautified their deeds to them, so they wander blindly. This is profound and heavy. At surface level, it means whatever evil they do appears good to them. But more subtly, whatever little good they do, they're incredibly proud of themselves for it. "I stopped at that stop sign for 2.5 seconds - citizen of the year!"

The word يَعْمَهُونَ means to be blind in the heart, to wander aimlessly. When the Afterlife disappears from consideration, moral blindness follows inevitably.

translation: Those are the ones for whom there will be the worst of punishment, and in the Hereafter they are the greatest losers
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلْعَذَابِ وَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمُ ٱلْأَخْسَرُونَ

Those are the ones for whom there is the worst punishment, and they will be the greatest losers in the Hereafter. The repetition emphasizes their state: هُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمُ ٱلْأَخْسَرُونَ - "they, in the Hereafter, they are the worst losers."

translation: And indeed, [O Muhammad], you receive the Qur'an from one Wise and Knowing
commentary:
وَإِنَّكَ لَتُلَقَّى ٱلْقُرْءَانَ مِن لَّدُنْ حَكِيمٍ عَلِيمٍ

Now Allah ﷻ addresses the Prophet ﷺ directly: "And indeed, you are receiving the Quran from the One who is Wise and Knowing." The word تُلَقَّى  implies coming into contact with something repeatedly, like a teacher spending time with a student until knowledge transfers completely. The student makes contact with the ayah again and again until it settles in their heart.

Notice the order of Allah's names here: حَكِيمٍ عَلِيمٍ - Wise and Knowing. Usually when these attributes appear together, knowledge comes first, then wisdom. Why wisdom first here? Because this Surah will discuss matters of the unseen where human minds reach their limits. Before appreciating His knowledge, we need to appreciate His wisdom. When we encounter things we don't understand, we need to remember: this comes from One far wiser than us.

translation: [Mention] when Moses said to his family, "Indeed, I have perceived a fire. I will bring you from there information or will bring you a burning torch that you may warm yourselves
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِأَهْلِهِۦٓ إِنِّىٓ ءَانَسْتُ نَارًا سَـَٔاتِيكُم مِّنْهَا بِخَبَرٍ أَوْ ءَاتِيكُم بِشِهَابٍ قَبَسٍ لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَصْطَلُونَ

The narrative shifts to Prophet Musa (AS): "When Musa said to his family, 'Indeed, I have perceived a fire. I will bring you from there information or bring you a burning torch that you may warm yourselves.'"

We've encountered this story before, but each retelling reveals new dimensions. The Quran presents these stories conversationally - remember when We told you this? Let Me tell you more today. This is how a teacher naturally refers back to previous lessons.

The word ءَانَسْتُ means to perceive something through clues, to sense something's presence. Musa (AS) noticed what appeared to be fire. In this version of the story, there's more confidence in his voice compared to other narrations. He doesn't say "perhaps" or "maybe" - he says definitively: سَـَٔاتِيكُم - "I will bring you." He's trying to reassure his nervous family left in the cold darkness.

The phrase شِهَابٍ قَبَسٍ describes a bright, sparkling flame to be borrowed. Musa (AS) sounds more certain he'll return with something useful - directions, fire for warmth, something to ease their situation.

translation: But when he came to it, he was called, "Blessed is whoever is at the fire and whoever is around it. And exalted is Allah, Lord of the worlds
commentary:
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهَا نُودِىَ أَنۢ بُورِكَ مَن فِى ٱلنَّارِ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا وَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

When he came to it, a call was made: "Blessed is whoever is in the fire and whoever is around it, and glory be to Allah, Lord of all the worlds."

In this telling, even his approach to the fire receives emphasis through the word فَلَمَّا جَآءَهَا - the strength of his coming is highlighted. There was a fire on a bush, and as the flames grew higher, the plant became greener and more full of life - the complete opposite of what fire normally does to vegetation.

The call announced: بُورِكَ مَن فِى ٱلنَّارِ - blessed is the one in the fire. Scholars debate this phrase. Some say it refers to the angel through whom Allah ﷻ was delivering the message. Others understand it as Allah ﷻ speaking through the medium of light or fire - but not fire that burns and destroys. This was fire giving life.

The immediate declaration سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ serves a critical purpose. When you see Allah ﷻ communicating through fire, you might begin associating Him ﷻ with fire. No - He is far above that. The fire is merely the means of communication. This is why SubhanAllah appears here - to deny any possibility of shirk before it can even form in the mind.

translation: O Moses, indeed it is I - Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّهُۥٓ أَنَا ٱللَّهُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

"O Musa, indeed it is I - Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Wise."

The names emphasize power and wisdom. The previous Surah emphasized العَزِيز (the Almighty) and الرَّحِيم (the Merciful). This Surah will highlight العَزِيز (the Almighty) and الحَكِيم (the Wise). From the beginning, wisdom is emphasized - appropriate for a Surah where we'll witness events that challenge common understanding.

translation: And [he was told], "Throw down your staff." But when he saw it writhing as if it were a snake, he turned in flight and did not return. [Allah said], "O Moses, fear not. Indeed, in My presence the messengers do not fear
commentary:
وَأَلْقِ عَصَاكَ ۚ فَلَمَّا رَءَاهَا تَهْتَزُّ كَأَنَّهَا جَآنٌّ وَلَّىٰ مُدْبِرًا وَلَمْ يُعَقِّبْ ۚ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ لَا تَخَفْ إِنِّى لَا يَخَافُ لَدَىَّ ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ

"Throw down your staff." When he saw it moving as if it were a serpent, he turned in flight and did not return. "O Musa, fear not. Indeed, the messengers do not fear in My presence."

The staff transforms, moving تَهْتَزُّ - vibrating, shaking, coming to life. The word جَآنّ (jann) can mean a type of snake, but also relates to life itself - something animated. Some historians note that jinn often took the form of snakes in folklore.

Musa's (AS) reaction is human and immediate: he ran without looking back - وَلَّىٰ مُدْبِرًا وَلَمْ يُعَقِّبْ. But Allah ﷻ reassures him: "Don't fear. Messengers don't fear in My presence."

Notice something remarkable: Allah ﷻ calls him a messenger before he even knows he's been chosen as one. "Those who have been sent don't need to fear." From the opening of this Surah, the theme shifts from warning to reassurance: don't fear, you're safe.

translation: Otherwise, he who wrongs, then substitutes good after evil - indeed, I am Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
إِلَّا مَن ظَلَمَ ثُمَّ بَدَّلَ حُسْنًۢا بَعْدَ سُوٓءٍ فَإِنِّى غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

"Except whoever has wronged, then substituted good after evil - indeed, I am Forgiving and Merciful."

Even those who do wrong have hope: if they replace evil with good, Allah ﷻ is Forgiving and Merciful. The word سُوء refers to purposeful, ugly wrong - strong terminology. The mention of this seems directed more broadly at people in general rather than at messengers specifically.

The construction is fascinating: messengers don't need to fear, but even regular people who've done terrible things shouldn't fear if they turn to Him ﷻ in repentance. For those who fear and turn back, Allah ﷻ is غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (Forgiving and Merciful). This isn't the time for fear of judgment - this is a time for mission.

translation: And put your hand into the opening of your garment [at the breast]; it will come out white without disease. [These are] among the nine signs [you will take] to Pharaoh and his people. Indeed, they have been a people defiantly disobedient
commentary:
وَأَدْخِلْ يَدَكَ فِى جَيْبِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضَآءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوٓءٍ ۖ فِى تِسْعِ ءَايَـٰتٍ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَقَوْمِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ

"Insert your hand into your chest pocket; it will come out white without disease - among nine signs to Pharaoh and his people. Indeed, they have been a disobedient people."

The word جَيْب refers to the chest area or chest pocket - in traditional garments, a large opening that went down to the armpit area. When Musa (AS) places his hand there and withdraws it, it emerges white - brilliant, radiant - مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوٓءٍ - without any defect or disease.

This miracle is among تِسْعِ ءَايَـٰت - nine signs sent to Pharaoh and his people. In previous Surahs, Allah ﷻ said "go with Our signs" (plural), creating a question: which signs? Now we receive the number: nine. Two are mentioned explicitly (the staff and the hand), leaving seven more.

The description ends with a judgment: إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ - they have been a people engaged in corruption for some time. Now it's time they receive this message.

translation: But when there came to them Our visible signs, they said, "This is obvious magic
commentary:
فَلَمَّا جَآءَتْهُمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا مُبْصِرَةً قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ

"When Our signs came to them, clear and visible, they said, 'This is obvious magic.'"

The narrative moves swiftly - no detailed back-and-forth. We get an overview. When the miraculous signs came مُبْصِرَةً - making things clearly visible - their response was dismissal: "This is magic."

This continues the theme from the previous Surah, where people consistently called clear signs "magic." It's as if Allah ﷻ is asking: what will you look at and not call magic?

translation: And they rejected them, while their [inner] selves were convinced thereof, out of injustice and haughtiness. So see how was the end of the corrupters
commentary:
وَجَحَدُوا۟ بِهَا وَٱسْتَيْقَنَتْهَآ أَنفُسُهُمْ ظُلْمًا وَعُلُوًّا ۚ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ

"They denied them, although their souls were convinced of them, out of injustice and haughtiness. So see how was the end of the corrupters."

This ayah contains devastating psychological insight. The word جَحَدُوا (jahadu) means to argue against something you know is wrong - to insist on denying truth you recognize internally. Meanwhile, وَٱسْتَيْقَنَتْهَآ أَنفُسُهُمْ - their inner selves gradually became convinced, day after day, through repeated experience.

The verb استيقان suggests gradual conviction over time. As the signs continued - frogs, locusts, blood - each one inched them closer to iman. At one point, Pharaoh's own generals turned to Musa (AS) and said, "Pray to your Lord to remove these signs from us." This admission reveals everything: they believed Musa (AS) was a messenger, that his God was powerful and capable of stopping the plagues. What else do you need to be Muslim?

Yet they remained in denial ظُلْمًا وَعُلُوًّا - out of wrongdoing and an urge to remain on top. Two addictions held them: addiction to wrongdoing itself, and addiction to their superior status. How could they give greatness to Allah ﷻ when they'd enjoyed that greatness for themselves for so long?

This teaches something profound: knowledge alone isn't iman. Look at Iblis. Does he deny Allah's existence? No - he knows Allah ﷻ exists. Does he deny revelation, messengers, the Day of Judgment? No - he knows all of it and will even quote what he'll say on Judgment Day. His kufr isn't denial of facts; it's ingratitude and rebellion. Knowledge must combine with character transformation. Until then, you create corruption.

See for yourselves the outcome of those who created corruption. The word عَـٰقِبَة when used with كَانَ specifically refers to punishment-outcome for those who spread corruption.

translation: And We had certainly given to David and Solomon knowledge, and they said, "Praise [is due] to Allah, who has favored us over many of His believing servants
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ وَسُلَيْمَـٰنَ عِلْمًا ۖ وَقَالَا ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى فَضَّلَنَا عَلَىٰ كَثِيرٍ مِّنْ عِبَادِهِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Now the narrative shifts to other messengers and their gifts: "We had certainly given Dawud and Sulaiman knowledge, and they said, 'Praise be to Allah, who has favored us over many of His believing servants.'"

We know little about Dawud (AS) from the Quran - glimpses of him helping Talut's armies, scattered references. But here we learn: both father and son received knowledge, and their first response was الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ.

Notice their specific wording: "who has favored us over many of His believing servants." They recognized so many other believers were worthy of these gifts. They didn't earn this - nobody earns Allah's gifts. Their choice became a matter of humility, not pride. This is the attitude every servant of Allah ﷻ should have when serving the deen. When you volunteer, teach, help at the masjid - don't think "I'm so much better than these people who do nothing." Think like Dawud and Sulaiman (AS): "Alhamdulillah that Allah ﷻ chose me and gave me the honor of serving Him, when so many others could have had this opportunity."

The underlying lesson reaches believers and disbelievers alike. Believers learn: when you have opportunities to serve Allah ﷻ, become more humble. Disbelievers are being reminded: you enjoy blessings - shouldn't they make you more humble rather than more arrogant? You were made custodians of the Kaaba, given custody of this sacred legacy when so many more powerful nations with histories of prophethood weren't given this honor. Why don't you think like that?

translation: And Solomon inherited David. He said, "O people, we have been taught the language of birds, and we have been given from all things. Indeed, this is evident bounty
commentary:
وَوَرِثَ سُلَيْمَـٰنُ دَاوُۥدَ ۖ وَقَالَ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ عُلِّمْنَا مَنطِقَ ٱلطَّيْرِ وَأُوتِينَا مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْفَضْلُ ٱلْمُبِينُ

"Sulaiman inherited from Dawud. He said, 'O people, we have been taught the language of birds, and we have been given from all things. Indeed, this is evident bounty.'"

The word وَرِثَ indicates inheritance - not just prophethood, but knowledge and skills. Dawud (AS) could speak with birds; this ability transferred to his son Sulaiman (AS).

Sulaiman (AS) makes a public announcement: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ (O people) - we've been taught the language of birds. What does this look like? How do birds speak? What vocabulary do they use? What's the grammar of bird communication? These are questions of Allah's wisdom. We may never understand the mechanism, but the fact remains: he was taught this.

وَأُوتِينَا مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ - we've been given all kinds of things. Then comes his conclusion: إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْفَضْلُ ٱلْمُبِينُ - this is absolutely, clearly, a huge favor from Allah ﷻ.

When someone declares something is فضل (favor), it implies: I must be grateful, and by extension, you must be grateful too. Sulaiman (AS) recognizes these gifts place him and his people under obligation of gratitude.

translation: And gathered for Solomon were his soldiers of the jinn and men and birds, and they were [marching] in rows
commentary:
وَحُشِرَ لِسُلَيْمَـٰنَ جُنُودُهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ وَٱلطَّيْرِ فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ

"And gathered for Sulaiman were his soldiers of jinn and men and birds, and they were organized in divisions."

Picture this: an army composed of three species - jinn, humans and birds - all assembled before him. The word يُوزَعُونَ means organized into battalions and rows. Hawks in one regiment, pigeons in another. Among jinn, the strong ones separated from the weaker ones. This massive parade of three distinct species, all marching in formation - it's almost beyond imagination. Yet people witnessed this.

translation: Until, when they came upon the valley of the ants, an ant said, "O ants, enter your dwellings that you not be crushed by Solomon and his soldiers while they perceive not
commentary:
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَتَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ وَادِ ٱلنَّمْلِ قَالَتْ نَمْلَةٌ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّمْلُ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ مَسَـٰكِنَكُمْ لَا يَحْطِمَنَّكُمْ سُلَيْمَـٰنُ وَجُنُودُهُۥ وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ

"Until when they came upon the valley of ants, an ant said, 'O ants, enter your dwellings that you not be crushed by Sulaiman and his soldiers while they perceive not.'"

This three-species army descends into a valley filled with ants - وَادِ ٱلنَّمْلِ. The Arabic indicates a female ant spoke - نَمْلَةٌ. Modern science confirms the queen communicates with and commands the colony.

Allah ﷻ zooms into ant-level perspective. As this massive army approaches, the queen broadcasts: "Listen up! Get inside your homes! Don't let Sulaiman and his soldiers crush you - and they won't even realize they've done it!"

The word يَحْطِمَنَّكُمْ means to crush and break things so completely you don't even feel the crunch - like dried leaves that crumble to dust underfoot.

Notice what the ant reveals: these ants understand who Sulaiman (AS) is. They comprehend that armies are approaching. They have intelligence, communication systems, a public broadcasting service where the presidential announcement comes from the queen. They know Sulaiman (AS) stands for justice - "They won't do it on purpose, they won't even realize."

SubhanAllah - all this about ants living in a valley somewhere. Next time you see ants crawling by, remember: they have conversations among themselves, and those conversations aren't insignificant to Allah ﷻ. He records them. If they're so small and insignificant to us yet Allah ﷻ records their conversations, what does that tell us about our own significance to Him ﷻ? How has He honored us by speaking directly with us?

translation: So [Solomon] smiled, amused at her speech, and said, "My Lord, enable me to be grateful for Your favor which You have bestowed upon me and upon my parents and to do righteousness of which You approve. And admit me by Your mercy into [the ranks of] Your righteous servants
commentary:
فَتَبَسَّمَ ضَاحِكًا مِّن قَوْلِهَا وَقَالَ رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِىٓ أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَىَّ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَىَّ وَأَنْ أَعْمَلَ صَـٰلِحًا تَرْضَىٰهُ وَأَدْخِلْنِى بِرَحْمَتِكَ فِى عِبَادِكَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

Now picture this: Sulaiman (AS) is descending a valley with armies making tremendous noise - thousands of footsteps. Yet despite all that sound, he heard the entire conversation between the ants.

He smiled - فَتَبَسَّمَ - and then couldn't hold the smile. He laughed - ضَاحِكًا. Even for someone commanding armies of jinn, humans and birds, this amazed him. The ants knew his name! They didn't call him a tyrant - they took precautions recognizing any harm would be accidental. It made him smile from her speech.

For someone with this much power and ability, the moment he realizes a new ability has been granted - instead of thinking "This is awesome, I'm going to have fun with this" - he immediately turns to Allah ﷻ: "My Master, inspire me to be grateful."

The word أَوْزِعْنِىٓ comes from وزع, which appears earlier in the same Surah referring to organizing armies into regiments. But أوزع means the opposite: to let flow forward, to inspire, to put motivation in someone's heart. He asks Allah ﷻ to put inspiration in his heart to constantly be grateful for the favor shown to him and his parents - that he should do righteous deeds in a way that pleases Allah ﷻ.

Notice the beautiful subtlety: صَـٰلِحًا تَرْضَىٰهُ - righteous deed that pleases You. The word صَالِح doesn't just cover what you do, but how you do it. You can pray - that's a good deed - but the way you prayed might not be pleasing to Allah ﷻ. Righteousness suggests both the action and the state of heart behind it. And the standard? تَرْضَىٰهُ - that You are pleased with it.

This matters especially for a king. He's marching with his armies, perhaps fulfilling Allah's command. But if even an ounce of arrogance enters his heart while doing this work, the outside deed may be good but the inside attitude is ugly and unacceptable. Religious service doesn't automatically mean acceptable work. There's the exterior of our deeds - teaching, building, volunteering - and there's the interior: the attitudes behind them.

Then he adds: وَأَدْخِلْنِى بِرَحْمَتِكَ فِى عِبَادِكَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ - "Enter me by Your mercy into the company of Your righteous servants." Earlier he mentioned so many other righteous believers could have been chosen. Now he's saying: when I leave this world - because he's aware he will die, unlike kings who cling to thrones till their last breath - I want to be in better company. Yes, I have a throne and following, but I want to be among the righteous.

His humility before Allah ﷻ is entirely separate from his position of authority over his people. He can be both a humble servant and a powerful king.

translation: And he took attendance of the birds and said, "Why do I not see the hoopoe - or is he among the absent
commentary:
وَتَفَقَّدَ ٱلطَّيْرَ فَقَالَ مَا لِىَ لَآ أَرَى ٱلْهُدْهُدَ أَمْ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْغَآئِبِينَ

"And he inspected the birds and said, 'Why do I not see the hoopoe - or is he among the absent?'"

The word تَفَقَّدَ means to search for something lost. Among an entire army of birds, Sulaiman (AS) notices one specific bird is missing - the hoopoe (الْهُدْهُدَ). He didn't just notice a bird was missing; he knew which bird.

His first reaction shows remarkable fairness: مَا لِىَ لَآ أَرَى - "What's wrong with me? Why don't I see?" He questions himself first before accusing the bird. Maybe his eyes aren't working correctly? Then: أَمْ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْغَآئِبِينَ - or is the hoopoe among those who are absent? Did he cut class?

This is an army. When a soldier skips formation in front of the general, there's trouble.

translation: I will surely punish him with a severe punishment or slaughter him unless he brings me clear authorization
commentary:
لَأُعَذِّبَنَّهُۥ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا أَوْ لَأَا۟ذْبَحَنَّهُۥٓ أَوْ لَيَأْتِيَنِّى بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ

"I will surely punish him severely or slaughter him, or he must bring me clear authorization."

Remember: three kinds of armies and one civilian population (the ants). Animals have animal rights, not civil rights. The hoopoe is a bird. Sulaiman (AS) has authority to slaughter his livestock just as anyone can. He knows how to exert appropriate authority over each regiment.

"I'm going to pluck his feathers" - torture him. "I'm going to slaughter him" - the hoopoe becomes lunch. "Or" - the only other possibility - "he better bring me clear proof, a clear excuse for why he didn't show up."

This bird is in serious trouble.

translation: But the hoopoe stayed not long and said, "I have encompassed [in knowledge] that which you have not encompassed, and I have come to you from Sheba with certain news
commentary:
فَمَكَثَ غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ فَقَالَ أَحَطتُ بِمَا لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِۦ وَجِئْتُكَ مِن سَبَإٍۭ بِنَبَإٍ يَقِينٍ

"But the hoopoe stayed not long and said, 'I have encompassed what you have not encompassed, and I have come to you from Sheba with certain news.'"

The word مَكَثَ means to wait in anticipation. There's this awkward scene - the entire army waiting. Where's the hoopoe? Everybody wants to see what happens because this is entertaining: the one in serious trouble facing the waiting authority.

He didn't wait long - فَمَكَثَ غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ. Smart bird. Opens his mouth immediately - no waiting around.

أَحَطتُ بِمَا لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِۦ - "I have acquired intelligence you don't even know about. I have gathered something you haven't." The bird shows up and panders: "I've got really important intelligence, sir!" وَجِئْتُكَ مِن سَبَإٍۭ بِنَبَإٍ يَقِينٍ - "I've come to you from Sheba with absolutely certain information. This is solid intelligence, not hearsay - verifiable."

translation: Indeed, I found [there] a woman ruling them, and she has been given of all things, and she has a great throne
commentary:
إِنِّى وَجَدتُّ ٱمْرَأَةً تَمْلِكُهُمْ وَأُوتِيَتْ مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَلَهَا عَرْشٌ عَظِيمٌ

"Indeed, I found a woman ruling them, and she has been given all things, and she has a great throne."

First thing mentioned: إِنِّى وَجَدتُّ ٱمْرَأَةً تَمْلِكُهُمْ - I found a woman ruling them. That's unusual, noteworthy to the hoopoe. Second: وَأُوتِيَتْ مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ - she's been given all kinds of resources. This implies massive kingdom, great power, abundant natural and human resources.

This is the Queen of Yemen - some of the most fertile land in Arabia, beautiful territory, a major trade capital. Then the bird adds: وَلَهَا عَرْشٌ عَظِيمٌ - she's got a huge, awesome throne.

Now imagine: he's telling this to a king. "She's got an awesome throne!" You tell a king somebody else has an awesome throne - what do you expect? "Excuse me, what was that?" But Sulaiman's (AS) reaction will be remarkable.

translation: I found her and her people prostrating to the sun instead of Allah, and Satan has made their deeds pleasing to them and averted them from [His] way, so they are not guided
commentary:
وَجَدتُّهَا وَقَوْمَهَا يَسْجُدُونَ لِلشَّمْسِ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ فَهُمْ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ

"I found her and her people prostrating to the sun instead of Allah, and Shaytan has made their deeds pleasing to them and averted them from the way, so they are not guided."

After describing the impressive kingdom, the hoopoe gets to the critical issue: وَجَدتُّهَا وَقَوْمَهَا يَسْجُدُونَ لِلشَّمْسِ - I found her and her people making sajdah to the sun مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ - instead of to Allah ﷻ.

Notice how the bird separates her from her nation: "her and her people." This separation is significant - the religion of the leader profoundly impacts the followers. Their lifestyle becomes the national lifestyle.

Then comes the explanation echoing the Surah's opening: وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ - Shaytan has beautified their deeds to them. Just as the beginning discussed those who don't believe in the Afterlife having their deeds beautified, here's a nation with so much more than the Quraysh - even a bird was impressed. Yet Shaytan beautified their deeds.

فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ - he's blocked them from the path. فَهُمْ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ - they are not committed to guidance, not holding onto it.

The parallels are clear: the Makkans enjoy gifts but turn away. Here's a nation with even greater gifts, equally turned away. The bird recognized this as critical intelligence worthy of bringing to Sulaiman (AS) - and worthy of saving his own feathers.

translation: [And] so they do not prostrate to Allah, who brings forth what is hidden within the heavens and the earth and knows what you conceal and what you declare
commentary:
أَلَّا يَسْجُدُوا۟ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى يُخْرِجُ ٱلْخَبْءَ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تُخْفُونَ وَمَا تُعْلِنُونَ

The hudhud (hoopoe bird) continues his report to Prophet Sulaiman (AS), explaining that the Queen and her people worship the sun instead of prostrating to Allah ﷻ. Notice how the bird carefully distinguishes between the Queen herself and her nation. This separation is significant because it teaches us that a leader's religion profoundly impacts their followers. The lifestyle and beliefs of those in authority ripple through their entire society.

The bird describes Allah ﷻ as the One "who brings out the hidden treasures" – الْخَبْءَ. The word khab' carries a fascinating meaning: it refers to something hidden that you ask people to find, like a riddle. More specifically, when used with the skies, it refers to treasures hidden there like rain and clouds. When used with the earth, it refers to treasures buried deep within it, like minerals and produce.

The Arabs would say: "The treasures of the sky brought out the treasures of the earth," meaning the rain finally came and produced vegetation. But these hidden treasures aren't limited to physical resources. The hidden treasures from the sky include revelation itself – divine guidance sent down from above.

Then the ayah states: "And He knows what you hide and what you declare." Why mention what is declared when it's obvious that if Allah ﷻ knows the hidden, He certainly knows what's open? The answer lies in highlighting a potential contradiction between the two. Allah ﷻ knows the intentions you keep hidden inside and the contradictory statements you make outwardly. This connects to what we learned in the previous passage about those who were internally convinced but still argued against the truth. What people say isn't always a true representation of what they hold inside.

translation: Allah - there is no deity except Him, Lord of the Great Throne
commentary:
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ رَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ ۩

The hudhud declares: "Allah – there is no deity except Him, the Lord of the Great Throne." Think about this moment: the Queen has an impressive throne that caught the bird's attention, yet his immediate comparison isn't to Sulaiman's throne but to Allah's Throne.

If we understand these words as coming from Sulaiman (AS) himself, which seems to be the case, then what humility Allah ﷻ teaches us through this prophet! Instead of feeling jealous or thinking about his own magnificent throne, Sulaiman (AS) immediately directs attention to the ultimate Throne – the Throne of Allah ﷻ. He doesn't say, "What about my throne?" or make any comparison to his own possessions. He bypasses himself entirely and goes straight to Allah ﷻ, affirming that the real Throne of authority belongs to the Master of all creation.

translation: [Solomon] said, "We will see whether you were truthful or were of the liars
commentary:
۞ قَالَ سَنَنظُرُ أَصَدَقْتَ أَمْ كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ

Sulaiman (AS) responds to the hudhud: "We will see whether you have told the truth or whether you are among the liars." This is a measured response. The bird has told quite a remarkable tale, and Sulaiman (AS) needs to verify it. After all, this is the same bird whose name appears on the execution list. Perhaps this elaborate story is just an attempt to redirect attention and avoid punishment.

The question is direct but fair: Have you spoken truthfully, or have you been making this up all along? This teaches us about leadership and accountability – even when receiving information that seems credible, verification is essential.

translation: Take this letter of mine and deliver it to them. Then leave them and see what [answer] they will return
commentary:
ٱذْهَب بِّكِتَـٰبِى هَـٰذَا فَأَلْقِهْ إِلَيْهِمْ ثُمَّ تَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ فَٱنظُرْ مَاذَا يَرْجِعُونَ

"Take this letter of mine and deliver it to them. Then turn away from them and watch what response they return with." Notice the diplomatic wisdom embedded in these instructions. Sulaiman (AS) tells the hudhud to deliver the letter and then step back – don't wait around for an immediate response. This is strategic communication. If you sit there waiting anxiously for their reaction, it makes you appear desperate. In diplomacy, you sometimes need to act unbothered, send your message and let it settle. Let them deliberate and respond on their own terms.

The use of فَأَلْقِهْ – "throw it" or "deliver it" – carries a tone of urgency and authority. There's even a hint of Sulaiman's lingering frustration with the hudhud in this command. Despite that, the strategy is sound: deliver the message, withdraw and observe their response.

This approach mirrors the Sunnah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, who sent letters to various rulers inviting them to Islam. However, this wasn't a blanket policy applied everywhere. The Prophet ﷺ sent letters to specific rulers whom Allah ﷻ had sanctioned him to contact – the Romans, the Persians and others who had already shown hostility or were regional powers that needed to be addressed.

The Sahaba (RA) who later traveled to distant lands like South India or China didn't necessarily go as conquerors threatening submission. They went as teachers, traders and examples of Islamic character. The military engagements that did occur were primarily with territories whose rulers had already received and rejected the Prophet's official letters – or had committed acts of war, like the Romans who killed a Muslim ambassador. This historical analysis is crucial for understanding how Islamic expansion actually worked versus how it's often misrepresented.

translation: She said, "O eminent ones, indeed, to me has been delivered a noble letter
commentary:
قَالَتْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَؤُا۟ إِنِّىٓ أُلْقِىَ إِلَىَّ كِتَـٰبٌ كَرِيمٌ

The letter arrives, delivered by a bird directly to the Queen – somehow bypassing all her security, all her guards, landing precisely in her hands. Imagine the shock: she's the most protected person in her civilization, yet this bird reaches her without any interception. A pigeon doesn't just show up at a presidential palace and drop a letter through the window, yet here it happened.

She immediately calls an emergency cabinet meeting and announces: "O assembly, indeed a noble letter has been delivered to me." Her choice of words is crucial. She doesn't say, "I've received a declaration of war" or "a threatening message" or "an offensive letter." She describes it as كَرِيمٌ – noble, gracious, respectful.

This single adjective tells us everything about her initial reaction. Before consulting with anyone, before any political calculation, she was impressed. The content, the tone, the miraculous delivery – all of it left an immediate positive impression on her. She's already leaning toward taking this message seriously.

translation: Indeed, it is from Solomon, and indeed, it reads: 'In the name of Allah, the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful
commentary:
إِنَّهُۥ مِن سُلَيْمَـٰنَ وَإِنَّهُۥ بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ

"Indeed, it is from Sulaiman, and indeed, it begins: 'In the name of Allah, the Extremely Merciful, the Always Merciful.'" She's certain of the letter's authenticity. She says إِنَّهُ – "It is absolutely from Sulaiman." This certainty could come from his name being on it, or perhaps they had already heard of Sulaiman and his extraordinary God-given powers, and the miraculous delivery confirmed it could only be him.

This ayah holds a unique distinction in the Quran: it's the only place where Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Rahim appears as part of an ayah's content rather than as a separate opening. This teaches us something profound – the phrase Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Rahim wasn't introduced for the first time with our Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. It was part of the revelation given to Sulaiman (AS) as well.

When the Queen and her people heard "Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Rahim," they should have recognized it as confirming their own scripture, their own prophetic tradition. This wasn't foreign theology being imposed on them; this was a return to the truth they once knew.

Notice also the diplomatic brilliance: when one king writes to another, the letter typically begins by asserting the sender's greatness, titles, achievements. But Sulaiman's letter begins by declaring his own humility before Allah ﷻ, acknowledging that the true King is the One who is infinitely merciful. The Queen found this deeply respectful and noble. Here was a king with immense power, yet his letter starts by establishing that he's a servant first, a king second.

translation: Be not haughty with me but come to me in submission [as Muslims]
commentary:
أَلَّا تَعْلُوا۟ عَلَىَّ وَأْتُونِى مُسْلِمِينَ

"Do not be haughty with me, and come to me in submission." After establishing the mercy of Allah ﷻ through the opening phrase, Sulaiman (AS) makes his point clear. The verb تَعْلُوا means to rise up against someone or to show arrogance toward them. There are two possible meanings here that both carry weight.

First, this could be a direct response to intelligence about territorial expansion. When the hudhud came rushing with news about this kingdom, it may not have been just about their impressive throne and sun worship. Perhaps there was urgent information that this nation was considering military advances into Sulaiman's territory. If that's the case, the first point of the letter is: "Stop right there. Don't make any aggressive moves toward me."

Second, and equally important, it means: "Don't respond to this message with arrogance. Don't let your pride or your status prevent you from accepting this truth." It's the opposite of being lowly (safil) – Sulaiman is telling them not to be high and mighty in their response.

Then comes the command: "Come to me as Muslims" – وَأْتُونِى مُسْلِمِينَ. This carries both spiritual and practical implications. Spiritually, come to me having submitted to Allah ﷻ, having accepted Islam. Practically, come to me in peace – don't come with your swords drawn, don't come with military formations. Come with your arms down, in a state of submission and surrender, not aggression.

Both dimensions of Islam – the religion and the act of peaceful submission – are being communicated here. This wasn't just an invitation to faith; it was also a diplomatic message about how any meeting between them would need to happen.

translation: She said, "O eminent ones, advise me in my affair. I would not decide a matter until you witness [for] me
commentary:
قَالَتْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَؤُا۟ أَفْتُونِى فِىٓ أَمْرِى مَا كُنتُ قَاطِعَةً أَمْرًا حَتَّىٰ تَشْهَدُونِ

The Queen addresses her advisors: "Give me your legal opinion in my matter. I would not make a decision in any matter until you are present as witnesses." The word she uses – أَفْتُونِى – deserves careful attention. A fatwa isn't just telling someone what to do; the root meaning includes empowerment. When you give a proper fatwa, you're empowering someone with both the right decision and the evidence behind it. You're giving them the tools to understand why this is the correct course of action.

This understanding elevates what we expect from Islamic scholarship. Muslims shouldn't just want to know "Do this" or "Don't do that." We should want to understand the reasoning, the evidence, the methodology. And scholars have a responsibility to explain themselves, to make their rationale accessible and subject to respectful questioning. This is what true empowerment through knowledge looks like.

The Queen demonstrates remarkable leadership qualities here. She says مَا كُنتُ قَاطِعَةً أَمْرًا – "I have never cut off discussion and made a unilateral decision." The verb قَطَعَ means to cut, and she's saying she has never cut her advisors out of the decision-making process. She doesn't make impulsive choices or bypass consultation. She waits for their testimony, their expert input.

This is consultation (shura) at its finest, and it's being demonstrated by a woman leader in a pre-Islamic civilization. The Quran presents this without any negative commentary about her gender or her role. Instead, we see wisdom, humility and proper governance.

translation: They said, "We are men of strength and of great military might, but the command is yours, so see what you will command
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ نَحْنُ أُو۟لُوا۟ قُوَّةٍ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ وَٱلْأَمْرُ إِلَيْكِ فَٱنظُرِى مَاذَا تَأْمُرِينَ

Her military advisors respond: "We possess strength, and we possess severe military might. But the command is yours, so see what you will command." These are military men, generals, and their first instinct is to highlight their capabilities. They're essentially saying, "We can handle war. We're not afraid of conflict. We have the power and the military strength to engage."

But here's what's remarkable: despite being men in positions of military power, despite perhaps having egos that would prefer to flex their strength, they defer completely to her authority. They don't say "We should go to war." They don't even use a verb suggesting action. They simply state their capability and then add: وَٱلْأَمْرُ إِلَيْكِ – "But the matter returns to you."

This shows the deep respect they have for her leadership. She has earned such trust and demonstrated such wisdom that even the military establishment, which could potentially challenge her authority, acknowledges that the final decision rests entirely with her. They will follow whatever command she issues.

Notice the contrast: the Queen was impressed by the letter's nobility and is leaning toward peace. The generals are talking about military capabilities but aren't pushing for war – they're leaving it completely open. This sets up a beautiful display of wisdom in the next ayah.

translation: She said, "Indeed kings - when they enter a city, they ruin it and render the honored of its people humbled. And thus do they do
commentary:
قَالَتْ إِنَّ ٱلْمُلُوكَ إِذَا دَخَلُوا۟ قَرْيَةً أَفْسَدُوهَا وَجَعَلُوٓا۟ أَعِزَّةَ أَهْلِهَآ أَذِلَّةً ۖ وَكَذَٰلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ

The Queen responds with a statement of historical and political wisdom: "Indeed, when kings enter a town after having conquered it, they corrupt it and humiliate its honored inhabitants. And thus do they do." This is her analysis based on observing patterns of conquest throughout history.

When an occupying force takes over a city, they engage in destruction – buildings are burned, civilians are killed, infrastructure is damaged. But beyond physical destruction, there's a systematic social restructuring. The people who were honored and respected in that society are deliberately humiliated and pushed to the margins. Meanwhile, those who were previously of low status but are willing to collaborate with the conquerors are elevated to positions of power and privilege.

We've seen this pattern repeat throughout history. When the British colonized India, Muslim scholars who were once respected community leaders found themselves unable to get jobs because they refused to learn English. They were pushed into certain neighborhoods, stripped of their former status. Meanwhile, those who collaborated with the British, who were willing to spy on their own people and serve colonial interests, were given high positions, generous salaries, properties and social status. The colonizers wanted to send a message: "The old order is gone. Those who serve us will prosper. Those who resist will be degraded."

This is exactly what happened with Bani Israel and Pharaoh. When we read about Pharaoh and his chiefs, we find that some Israelites like Qarun were given wealth and authority. How could a slave nation produce someone so wealthy? Because Pharaoh needed collaborators from within that community, people who could keep watch over their own people and maintain control. This strategy of inverting social hierarchies is how occupying powers maintain dominance.

The Queen understands this dynamic perfectly. She's saying: "Whether we conquer them or they conquer us, this pattern of corruption and social destruction will follow. War has terrible consequences that go beyond the battlefield."

translation: But indeed, I will send to them a gift and see with what [reply] the messengers will return
commentary:
وَإِنِّى مُرْسِلَةٌ إِلَيْهِم بِهَدِيَّةٍ فَنَاظِرَةٌۢ بِمَ يَرْجِعُ ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ

"And I am going to send them a gift, and I will wait and see with what response the messengers return." This is her diplomatic strategy. She announces: إِنِّى مُرْسِلَةٌ – using the emphatic noun form to indicate this is a firm decision, not open to debate anymore.

Sulaiman (AS) had called her to Islam, which means accepting any bribe or gift as a way to avoid addressing the religious call would be an insult to the message. But she wants to test him. She's testing his character, his priorities, his sincerity. Is this really about faith and guidance, or is this about power and resources?

She's being strategic: "Let's not take any military action yet. Let's send them a generous gift" – and it would be generous given that she has been given all kinds of wealth and resources. "Let's send ambassadors with this gift and wait to see how they respond. Their response will tell us who we're really dealing with."

This is intelligent leadership. Before making any irreversible decisions, gather more information. Test their intentions. See if this is truly about a spiritual message or if it's a political maneuver that can be negotiated with material offerings.

translation: So when they came to Solomon, he said, "Do you provide me with wealth? But what Allah has given me is better than what He has given you. Rather, it is you who rejoice in your gift
commentary:
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ قَالَ أَتُمِدُّونَنِ بِمَالٍ فَمَآ ءَاتَىٰنِۦَ ٱللَّهُ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّآ ءَاتَىٰكُم بَلْ أَنتُم بِهَدِيَّتِكُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ

When the ambassadors arrive with their impressive gifts, Sulaiman's response is immediate and powerful: "Are you trying to extend my wealth with money? What Allah ﷻ has given me is far better than what He has given you." Notice again his beautiful humility – he doesn't say "What I have is better than what you have." He attributes everything to Allah ﷻ: "What Allah has given me is better than what He has given you."

This shows profound understanding. Even what they possess isn't truly theirs – Allah ﷻ gave it to them. And now they're trying to give back to Sulaiman (AS) what ultimately came from Allah ﷻ in the first place. He's saying, "You think you can impress me with this? Allah has already given me more than you can imagine."

Then comes the sharp observation: بَلْ أَنتُم بِهَدِيَّتِكُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ – "Rather, you are the ones who rejoice in your gift." The word تَفْرَحُونَ indicates they came with excessive pride and joy, thinking their gift was so magnificent it would surely win him over. Perhaps they arrived with grand ceremony, announcing their generous offering with flourish and confidence.

Sulaiman (AS) sees through it immediately. You're so proud of this gift, so confident it will work. You think you can buy your way out of the real issue. But this isn't about trade negotiations or political alliances that can be purchased. This is about submitting to Allah ﷻ, and that can't be bought with any amount of wealth.

translation: Return to them, for we will surely come to them with soldiers that they will be powerless to encounter, and we will surely expel them therefrom in humiliation, and they will be debased
commentary:
ٱرْجِعْ إِلَيْهِمْ فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّهُم بِجُنُودٍ لَّا قِبَلَ لَهُم بِهَا وَلَنُخْرِجَنَّهُم مِّنْهَآ أَذِلَّةً وَهُمْ صَـٰغِرُونَ

Now Sulaiman (AS) gets firm. "Return to them, and we will surely come to them with soldiers they cannot withstand, and we will expel them from it humiliated while they are lowly." The diplomatic phase is over. They tried to turn a spiritual call into a political negotiation, and that won't be tolerated.

The emphatic oaths pile up: لَنَأْتِيَنَّهُم – "We will absolutely, definitely come to them." This is a done deal. We're coming with جُنُودٍ – armies. And we've already learned that Sulaiman (AS) commands three types of forces: human soldiers, jinn and birds. His firepower literally includes creatures made of fire.

لَّا قِبَلَ لَهُم بِهَا – "They have no ability to face this." They cannot match this kind of force. They have never encountered anything like what's coming.

وَلَنُخْرِجَنَّهُم – "And we will drive them out." Why? Because they haven't used the land to establish justice. They've used it to build their kingdom on shirk, on sun worship, on false deities. The land they occupy has been corrupted by their disbelief.

أَذِلَّةً وَهُمْ صَـٰغِرُونَ – "Humiliated and brought low." They will leave in disgrace, stripped of their arrogance, experiencing the very humiliation the Queen described that conquering kings inflict. But this isn't mere conquest for power – this is divine judgment against those who rejected the call to truth after it reached them clearly.

translation: [Solomon] said, "O assembly [of jinn], which of you will bring me her throne before they come to me in submission
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَؤُا۟ أَيُّكُمْ يَأْتِينِى بِعَرْشِهَا قَبْلَ أَن يَأْتُونِى مُسْلِمِينَ

After sending the ambassadors back with that severe message, Sulaiman (AS) turns to his own cabinet meeting. Just as the Queen consulted her chiefs, now Sulaiman (AS) consults his. And we learn something important here: among his close advisors are jinn.

He says: "O assembly, which of you can bring me her throne before they come to me in submission?" Notice his confidence – he doesn't say "before they come to fight" or "before they attack." He says قَبْلَ أَن يَأْتُونِى مُسْلِمِينَ – before they come as Muslims, in submission.

He's so certain that his last message will work, that they won't come with military aggression. They will come in Islam, in surrender to Allah's command. But before they arrive, he wants something: the throne that so impressed the hoopoe bird, the throne he heard so much about. He wants it brought to his palace.

Why? The Queen wanted to test him with a gift. Now he wants to test her. When she arrives, he wants to modify her throne slightly and see if she recognizes it. It's almost playful – he's planning a practical test of her intelligence and perception. Let's see how sharp she really is.

translation: A powerful one from among the jinn said, "I will bring it to you before you rise from your place, and indeed, I am for this [task] strong and trustworthy
commentary:
قَالَ عِفْرِيتٌ مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ أَنَا۠ ءَاتِيكَ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ أَن تَقُومَ مِن مَّقَامِكَ ۖ وَإِنِّى عَلَيْهِ لَقَوِىٌّ أَمِينٌ

An 'ifrit from among the jinn immediately volunteers: "I will bring it to you before you rise from your place, and indeed, I am strong and trustworthy for this task." The word عِفْرِيتٌ is powerful and specific. Before I studied the Quran seriously, I first heard this word in a video game – Final Fantasy VII, where players could summon powerful demons to fight on their behalf. When I later studied Quran and encountered this word, I was shocked at how far the term had traveled.

But what does 'ifrit actually mean? The word comes from roots related to raising dust and dirt. In Arabic culture, when horses race and one is winning, it kicks up dust that clouds the vision of the horses behind it. Sometimes a winning rider would deliberately guide his horse to kick dust into opponents' faces, disorienting them and disrupting the race. This aggressive, dominant victory earned the title 'ifrit.

So an 'ifrit among jinn refers to a powerful, muscular jinn – one who crushes his opponents, humiliates them, makes them "bite the dust." He's not just strong; he's a bully, someone who dominates. This particular 'ifrit volunteers and says: أَنَا۠ ءَاتِيكَ بِهِۦ – "I will bring it to you" – قَبْلَ أَن تَقُومَ مِن مَّقَامِكَ – "before you adjourn your court, before this cabinet meeting is over."

Then he adds his qualifications: وَإِنِّى عَلَيْهِ لَقَوِىٌّ أَمِينٌ – "I'm strong enough for this task, and you can trust me." The throne is delicate, intricate. Raw strength isn't enough – you need to handle it carefully. So he assures Sulaiman (AS): I have the muscle to move it, and I have the trustworthiness to deliver it intact. You can count on me.

He wants to show off his strength, to prove his value in the court. But someone else is about to make an even more extraordinary offer.

translation: Said one who had knowledge from the Scripture, "I will bring it to you before your glance returns to you." And when [Solomon] saw it placed before him, he said, "This is from the favor of my Lord to test me whether I will be grateful or ungrateful. And whoever is grateful - his gratitude is only for [the benefit of] himself. And whoever is ungrateful - then indeed, my Lord is Free of need and Generous
commentary:
قَالَ ٱلَّذِى عِندَهُۥ عِلْمٌ مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَنَا۠ ءَاتِيكَ بِهِۦ قَبْلَ أَن يَرْتَدَّ إِلَيْكَ طَرْفُكَ ۚ فَلَمَّا رَءَاهُ مُسْتَقِرًّا عِندَهُۥ قَالَ هَـٰذَا مِن فَضْلِ رَبِّى لِيَبْلُوَنِىٓ ءَأَشْكُرُ أَمْ أَكْفُرُ ۖ وَمَن شَكَرَ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ رَبِّى غَنِىٌّ كَرِيمٌ

"The one who had knowledge from the Book said: 'I will bring it to you before your glance returns to you.'" This is one of the most mysterious and beautiful moments in the Quran. Someone described only as "the one who had knowledge from the Book" makes an incredible offer. Allah ﷻ doesn't tell us who this person was, and that ambiguity is itself wisdom – we trust Allah's wisdom in what He reveals and what He conceals.

Some scholars suggest this was a righteous person in Sulaiman's court who had been granted special knowledge. Others say this was Sulaiman (AS) himself, speaking after the 'ifrit volunteered, as if to say: "You think you're strong? Watch this." But if it was Sulaiman himself, why would he say "I will bring it to you"? Some explain he might have been addressing the 'ifrit, demonstrating superior capability.

Regardless of the identity, the timeframe is astounding: قَبْلَ أَن يَرْتَدَّ إِلَيْكَ طَرْفُكَ – "before your gaze returns to you" – before you blink, before you lower and raise your eyes, before you can move your eyeballs. In that fraction of a second, the throne would be there.

فَلَمَّا رَءَاهُ مُسْتَقِرًّا عِندَهُۥ – "And when he saw it settled before him" – possibly the 'ifrit witnessed this and stood there stunned – the throne materialized instantly.

Now, I'm inclined to believe the response that follows is from Sulaiman (AS) himself, not the unnamed person, because of what's said next: هَـٰذَا مِن فَضْلِ رَبِّى – "This is from the favor of my Lord." Once again, Sulaiman demonstrates extraordinary humility. He just witnessed an incredible miracle, something that should make him proud of his kingdom and the remarkable people in his service. His court includes individuals who can accomplish the seemingly impossible.

But he doesn't say, "Look at what my people can do! Look at how powerful my court is! We're the strongest kingdom!" No. He immediately attributes it to Allah ﷻ: "This is from my Lord's favor." And then he adds the most beautiful reflection: لِيَبْلُوَنِىٓ – "So He can test me."

ءَأَشْكُرُ أَمْ أَكْفُرُ – "Will I be grateful, or will I be ungrateful?" He recognizes this as a test. The moment you're given something extraordinary, Allah ﷻ is watching: What will you do with it? Will you recognize it as a gift from Him and remain humble? Or will you become proud and claim credit for yourself?

This is a pattern throughout Sulaiman's story. Every time something happens that could make him arrogant – whether it's understanding the speech of animals, commanding the wind, having jinn in his service, or now seeing this miraculous transportation of a throne – he immediately turns to Allah ﷻ in humility and says, "This is a test. Will I be grateful or not?"

Then he teaches us about gratitude: وَمَن شَكَرَ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِۦ – "And whoever is grateful, he is only grateful for his own benefit." When you thank Allah ﷻ, you're not doing Him any favors. He doesn't need your gratitude. Your gratitude benefits you – it keeps your heart right, it maintains your connection to Him, it protects you from arrogance.

وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ رَبِّى غَنِىٌّ كَرِيمٌ – "And whoever is ungrateful, then indeed my Lord is Self-Sufficient and Noble." If I show even a hint of ingratitude, if I become proud and think this is all because of me, Allah ﷻ doesn't need me. He is غَنِىّ – completely independent, in need of nothing. He is كَرِيم – Noble and Generous.

The implication is powerful: He could take all of this away. He could remove my kingdom, strip away these blessings. I am completely dispensable before Allah ﷻ. He doesn't need me to spread His message. He doesn't need my kingdom to establish Islam. He could do it all on His own. I better never act like I'm indispensable, like He needs me. This continuous awareness of his own insignificance before Allah ﷻ is what made Sulaiman (AS) truly great.

translation: He said, "Disguise for her her throne; we will see whether she will be guided [to truth] or will be of those who is not guided
commentary:
قَالَ نَكِّرُوا۟ لَهَا عَرْشَهَا نَنظُرْ أَتَهْتَدِىٓ أَمْ تَكُونُ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ

Now that the throne has arrived, Sulaiman (AS) gives an interesting instruction: "Alter her throne for her. Let's see if she will be guided, or if she will be among those who are not guided." The word نَكِّرُوا means to make something unfamiliar, to disguise it, to change it slightly so it's not immediately recognizable.

So he tells them: modify it a bit – maybe remove that decorative piece on top, change the color of this section, switch out the cushions, alter some details. Make it look similar but different enough that it requires real intelligence to identify.

Then he explains why: نَنظُرْ أَتَهْتَدِىٓ أَمْ تَكُونُ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ – "Let's see if she will be guided or if she will be among those who don't get guided." The word "guided" here refers to her ability to figure it out, to recognize her own throne despite the alterations. Is she sharp enough? Does she know her throne well enough? Can she see through the modifications?

This is a test of her intelligence and perception. The Queen wanted to test Sulaiman with a gift. Now he's testing her with her own throne. It's almost playful, but it's also strategic – he wants to see what kind of mind he's dealing with.

translation: So when she arrived, it was said [to her], "Is your throne like this?" She said, "[It is] as though it was it." [Solomon said], "And we were given knowledge before her, and we have been Muslims [in submission to Allah]
commentary:
فَلَمَّا جَآءَتْ قِيلَ أَهَـٰكَذَا عَرْشُكِ ۖ قَالَتْ كَأَنَّهُۥ هُوَ ۚ وَأُوتِينَا ٱلْعِلْمَ مِن قَبْلِهَا وَكُنَّا مُسْلِمِينَ

When she arrived, the question was posed carefully: أَهَـٰكَذَا عَرْشُكِ – "Does your throne look like this?" Notice the diplomatic brilliance of this phrasing. They didn't ask "Is this your throne?" which would be a simpler yes-or-no question and would essentially give away the answer.

Instead, they asked: "Does your throne look like this?" This is harder. It implies: this isn't your throne, but it looks similar to yours. So if she simply says "Yes, it looks just like that," she's failed the test – she didn't catch on to what actually happened.

Her response is masterful: قَالَتْ كَأَنَّهُۥ هُوَ – "She said: It is as if it is the same one." Not "it is" – but "it's as if it is." She's giving a diplomatic, intelligent answer that doesn't commit her to a definitive statement. She's essentially saying: "This looks almost exactly like mine. It's so similar I think it might actually be mine, but I'm not going to directly accuse you of having taken my throne, because I can't be absolutely certain. But it's remarkably, remarkably close."

This impresses them. She gave an answer that's smart enough to show she recognized the similarities, but diplomatic enough not to make accusations. She passed the test.

Then she says something profound: وَأُوتِينَا ٱلْعِلْمَ مِن قَبْلِهَا وَكُنَّا مُسْلِمِينَ – "We were given knowledge before this, and we were already Muslims." She's speaking on behalf of herself and her delegation, perhaps turning to address Sulaiman (AS) directly.

She's saying: "You didn't need to do this to convince us. This demonstration of power, showing us you can bring my throne here – it's impressive, it's incredible, and yes, I'm in awe of what Allah ﷻ has enabled you to do. But we had already received knowledge before we came. We were already convinced by your letter. We had already submitted to Islam. This was icing on the cake. It's very cool that you did it, but it was overkill."

This reveals something beautiful about her character. She wasn't someone who needed to be dazzled by miracles. The intellectual and spiritual truth in the message itself was enough. The letter, the call to Allah ﷻ, the reasoning – that had already reached her heart. The throne demonstration certainly reinforced everything, but her Islam wasn't dependent on seeing supernatural proofs.

translation: And that which she was worshipping other than Allah had averted her [from submission to Him]. Indeed, she was from a disbelieving people
commentary:
وَصَدَّهَا مَا كَانَت تَّعْبُدُ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهَا كَانَتْ مِن قَوْمٍ كَـٰفِرِينَ

There's another reading of the previous passage that's worth considering. The phrase وَصَدَّهَا means "and it prevented her" or "blocked her." Some scholars say this refers to the throne itself – that witnessing the throne miraculously transported did prevent her from continuing in her previous worship practices.

مَا كَانَت تَّعْبُدُ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ – "from what she used to worship besides Allah." In other words, seeing this manifestation of Allah's power completely eliminated whatever remnants of shirk might have lingered in her heart. The demonstration wasn't overkill after all – it served a purpose. It fully purified her belief.

Because there's a difference between intellectual conviction and experiential conviction. You can know something is true, but when you witness it, when you see it with your own eyes, it reaches a different level. This is the difference between 'ilm al-yaqin (knowledge of certainty), 'ayn al-yaqin (eye of certainty), and haqq al-yaqin (truth of certainty).

We know there's Paradise and Hellfire. We believe in them intellectually. But when the Prophet ﷺ was shown Paradise and Hellfire during the Night Journey, his level of conviction became incomparable to ours. Seeing is not like hearing. Being there is not like reading about it.

So when she witnessed this miracle with her own eyes, any doors to shirk that might have still been slightly ajar were slammed shut. إِنَّهَا كَانَتْ مِن قَوْمٍ كَـٰفِرِينَ – "Indeed, she had been from a disbelieving people." She came from a nation steeped in shirk, in sun worship. That background, that upbringing, those cultural influences – they can leave traces. But now, after this experience, those traces were completely erased.

translation: She was told, "Enter the palace." But when she saw it, she thought it was a body of water and uncovered her shins [to wade through]. He said, "Indeed, it is a palace [whose floor is] made smooth with glass." She said, "My Lord, indeed I have wronged myself, and I submit with Solomon to Allah, Lord of the worlds
commentary:
قِيلَ لَهَا ٱدْخُلِى ٱلصَّرْحَ ۖ فَلَمَّا رَأَتْهُ حَسِبَتْهُ لُجَّةً وَكَشَفَتْ عَن سَاقَيْهَا ۚ قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ صَرْحٌ مُّمَرَّدٌ مِّن قَوَارِيرَ ۗ قَالَتْ رَبِّ إِنِّى ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِى وَأَسْلَمْتُ مَعَ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

The final test comes as she's invited to enter: قِيلَ لَهَا ٱدْخُلِى ٱلصَّرْحَ – "It was said to her: Enter the palace." The word صَرْح refers to a distinguished, lofty building that's free of impurities and beautifully decorated on the outside. Think of a towering structure with intricate architectural details, ornate facades, artistic elements that make it visually stunning.

She's standing outside looking up at this magnificent tower, already impressed by its exterior beauty and craftsmanship. Then Sulaiman (AS) says: "This is nothing. You haven't seen anything yet. Come inside."

فَلَمَّا رَأَتْهُ حَسِبَتْهُ لُجَّةً – "When she saw it, she thought it was the deepest part of the ocean." The word لُجَّة specifically means the deepest portion of the ocean, the darkest depths. She walks in the door and looks down, and she sees what appears to be deep water with fish swimming beneath her feet. Her immediate thought: I'm about to step into the ocean.

وَكَشَفَتْ عَن سَاقَيْهَا – "So she raised her garments" – she immediately lifted her dress or robe to keep it from getting wet, exposing her ankles and lower legs in preparation to wade through water.

قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ صَرْحٌ مُّمَرَّدٌ مِّن قَوَارِيرَ – "He said: No, no, this is actually a palace with a smooth floor made of glass." The word مُّمَرَّد means completely smooth, polished to perfection. It comes from the same root that describes someone who can't grow facial hair – perfectly smooth. Or when you take a branch with all its sub-branches and leaves and you shave them all off until it's just a smooth stick – that's mumarrad.

The interior floor of this palace was made of perfectly smooth, clear glass or crystal – so smooth you couldn't see any imperfections, any scratches, any stains. And beneath that glass floor was an aquarium. The aquarium wasn't on the side wall where you'd expect it. You were walking on top of it. The floor itself was the ceiling of an aquarium, and it was so flawlessly transparent that it looked exactly like water. That's why she thought she was about to step into the ocean.

Imagine the architectural sophistication required for this. This isn't just wealth – this is advanced engineering and artistic vision. The Queen, who thought she had seen everything, who ruled over a wealthy kingdom herself, is stunned. And she realizes something in that moment.

Every gift Sulaiman (AS) has shown her – the ability to communicate with birds, the throne mysteriously transported, and now this architectural marvel – none of it is about impressing her with his own greatness. It's about showing her the greatness of Allah ﷻ. The more impressed she becomes with what's in his kingdom, the more she's actually being impressed with what Allah ﷻ can give. She thought she had a kingdom, but this is on another level entirely. And she understands: this is why he spoke with such confidence in his letter. This is why he said "What Allah has given me is better than what He gave you."

And in that moment of realization, she makes her declaration: قَالَتْ رَبِّ إِنِّى ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِى – "She said: My Lord, I have certainly wronged myself." This is her confession. She doesn't make excuses, doesn't try to justify her past. She owned a throne, she ruled a nation, she was worshipped and honored, but she got the most fundamental thing wrong. She wronged her own soul by not submitting to Allah ﷻ sooner.

وَأَسْلَمْتُ مَعَ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ – "And I submit with Sulaiman to Allah, Lord of all the worlds." Notice she says "with Sulaiman" – they're both servants together, both submitting to the same Master. She doesn't say "I submit to Sulaiman." She understands the hierarchy clearly: Sulaiman is a servant-king, and both of them are submitting to Allah ﷻ, the true King of everything.

The connection here is profound. If we came to this kingdom because we thought this king was powerful, and we were prepared to submit to his authority, how much more should we submit to Allah ﷻ? If we realized we had no match against Sulaiman, how can we think we have any measure against Allah ﷻ? Her military advisors had said "We have strength, we have might" – but she wisely chose peace. Now she sees that even Sulaiman's incredible power is nothing compared to Allah's power. And if she had to humble herself before a human king, how much more should she humble herself before the Lord of all creation?

This realization – that worldly power should make us more aware of Allah's power, not distract us from it – is at the heart of these stories. The gifts of this world should bring us closer to Allah ﷻ, not take us further from Him.

translation: And We had certainly sent to Thamud their brother Salih, [saying], "Worship Allah," and at once they were two parties conflicting
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَإِذَا هُمْ فَرِيقَانِ يَخْتَصِمُونَ

Now the Quran shifts to another nation. Remember, there are always two stories being told in these narratives: the one being recounted and the one unfolding in Makkah. The Quraysh enjoyed tremendous gifts – they controlled the Ka'bah, they had trade routes, they had security and prosperity. Those gifts should have brought them closer to Allah ﷻ, but instead, they turned them away from Him.

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا – "And We had certainly sent to Thamud their brother Salih." The word أَخَاهُمْ (akhahum) – "their brother" – emphasizes that he was one of them, from their own people, speaking their language, understanding their culture. He wasn't a foreigner making demands; he was their own brother calling them to truth.

أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ – with the message "Worship Allah." That's it. The core message is always the same: turn to Allah ﷻ alone, abandon false worship, submit to your Creator.

فَإِذَا هُمْ فَرِيقَانِ يَخْتَصِمُونَ – "And suddenly they became two parties arguing." As soon as the message came, the nation split into two groups engaged in heated debate and conflict. This mirrors exactly what happened in Makkah. The moment the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ called to Islam, the Quraysh divided – some accepted, others rejected, and the conflict began.

translation: He said, "O my people, why are you impatient for evil instead of good? Why do you not seek forgiveness of Allah that you may receive mercy
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لِمَ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ قَبْلَ ٱلْحَسَنَةِ ۖ لَوْلَا تَسْتَغْفِرُونَ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ

Salih (AS) addresses his people: "O my people, why do you rush toward evil before good?" The word تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ means to hurry, to rush toward something impatiently. They were rushing toward what's harmful, toward what's bad for them.

This could mean several things. Perhaps they were mockingly demanding: "Bring the punishment you're warning us about! We're not going to change, so just bring it already!" This is similar to when the Quraysh would taunt the Prophet ﷺ saying "Where's this punishment you keep talking about?" They rush toward their own destruction through their arrogance and defiance.

It could also mean they were rushing after this worldly life and its fleeting pleasures, chasing after evil desires, before even considering doing good. Why are you always focused on the temporary, the harmful, the shallow, before you think about what's actually beneficial?

لَوْلَا تَسْتَغْفِرُونَ ٱللَّهَ – "Why don't you just ask Allah for forgiveness?" This is such a compassionate appeal. He's not threatening them, not condemning them. He's saying: why don't you simply turn to Allah ﷻ and seek His forgiveness?

لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ – "So that you might receive mercy." Maybe you'll be shown mercy. Maybe Allah ﷻ will forgive you and lift the difficulties you're facing. It's not too late. The door is still open.

translation: They said, "We consider you a bad omen, you and those with you." He said, "Your omen is with Allah. Rather, you are a people being tested
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ ٱطَّيَّرْنَا بِكَ وَبِمَن مَّعَكَ ۚ قَالَ طَـٰٓئِرُكُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ تُفْتَنُونَ

Their response reveals their mindset: قَالُوا۟ ٱطَّيَّرْنَا بِكَ وَبِمَن مَّعَكَ – "They said: We consider you a bad omen, you and those with you." The word ٱطَّيَّرْنَا comes from طَيْر, meaning bird, but it evolved to mean considering something cursed or a bad omen.

Many cultures have superstitions about certain birds bringing bad luck. The ancient Arabs had a practice where they'd grab a bird, throw it in the air, and make decisions based on which direction it flew. If it went right, that was a good sign; if it went left, that was bad luck. Over time, birds became associated with omens, and the verb from this root came to mean "we think you're cursed."

They're saying: "Ever since you came, we've had problems. Our nation has split into two groups. We're being tested with famine and difficulty. You're the reason for our hardships. You and your followers are cursed."

This is a common response to prophets. When people face trials after a messenger comes, instead of seeing it as a test meant to soften their hearts and bring them closer to Allah ﷻ, they blame the messenger. "Things were fine before you showed up with your message."

قَالَ طَـٰٓئِرُكُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ – "He said: Your 'bird' is with Allah." Your fortune, your fate, your omen – whatever you want to call it – is with Allah ﷻ. He's the One who controls what happens to you. He knows the reality of your situation. This isn't about me being bad luck.

بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ تُفْتَنُونَ – "Rather, you are a people being tested." The word تُفْتَنُونَ has multiple meanings. It means you're going through a severe trial, a difficult test. It also carries the meaning of being deluded, being led astray by your own misconceptions. You're being tested, and you're failing that test by blaming others instead of recognizing it as an opportunity to turn to Allah ﷻ.

translation: And there were in the city nine family heads causing corruption in the land and not amending [its affairs]
commentary:
وَكَانَ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ تِسْعَةُ رَهْطٍ يُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا يُصْلِحُونَ

Now the Quran introduces a specific element of corruption within Thamud: وَكَانَ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ تِسْعَةُ رَهْطٍ 3– "And there were in the city nine gangs" or "nine groups of men." The word رَهْط refers to a group, typically between three and nine men, though some say up to forty. But the word رَهْط is specifically used for troublemakers, for people who band together for corrupt purposes.

يُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا يُصْلِحُونَ – "who caused corruption in the land and did not amend." They were spreading fasad – corruption, mischief, destruction – and they made no effort whatsoever to do anything good or constructive. Their entire existence was about causing problems.

This parallels the situation in Makkah. There were multiple powerful families in Makkah – Banu Hashim, Banu Makhzum, Banu Umayya and others. Not all of them were united, but they found common ground in opposing the Prophet ﷺ. Similarly, in Thamud, there were nine influential groups who would become key players in a dark conspiracy.

translation: They said, "Take a mutual oath by Allah that we will kill him by night, he and his family. Then we will say to his executor, 'We did not witness the destruction of his family, and indeed, we are truthful
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ تَقَاسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ لَنُبَيِّتَنَّهُۥ وَأَهْلَهُۥ ثُمَّ لَنَقُولَنَّ لِوَلِيِّهِۦ مَا شَهِدْنَا مَهْلِكَ أَهْلِهِۦ وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ

These nine groups held a secret meeting and made a sinister pact: قَالُوا۟ تَقَاسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ – "They said: Swear to each other by Allah." The verb تَقَاسَمُوا is significant. It comes from قَسَم, which means to swear an oath. But the form تَقَاسَمُوا indicates mutual, cooperative swearing – they're all swearing together, supporting each other's oath, binding themselves to each other.

The word also carries a connotation of قَسَامَة, which means to repeatedly swear when someone doesn't believe you, to keep insisting with oaths until they accept your testimony. There's friction implied in this form – you're struggling to convince someone, so you keep piling on oaths. This is the same form used when Iblis swore to Adam (AS): لَقَاسَمَهُمَا – "he swore to them," repeatedly insisting he meant well until they believed him.
